ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based snack / confectionery — glutinous rice cake
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Ang Ku Kueh. A0CKP (rice-based snack) is the closest defensible anchor. Sweet bean filling variant is not separately coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Ang Ku Kueh or glutinous rice cake with sweet bean filling as of 2026.

allergens: gluten (trace — glutinous rice flour may be processed in facilities with wheat)
soy (mung bean paste may contain soy)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of Ang Ku Kueh weighs approximately 45 g, providing about 94 kcal, 16.7 g carbohydrate, and 5.0 g sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Ang Ku Kueh (Sweet Bean) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Ang Ku Kueh (Sweet Bean), CR ID F11090003. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous glutinous rice cake entries as HPB does not report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; glutinous rice / waxy rice entries
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ang Ku Kueh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GI of 98 is assigned based on the well-established GI of glutinous (waxy) rice reported in the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2008; glucose reference = 100). Glutinous rice flour is the dominant carbohydrate component of the skin. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (98 × 16.7) / 100 ≈ 16.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.2 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g); sodium is low at 64 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 64 mg/100 g is within acceptable limits for hypertension, but the high sugar content (11.2 g/100 g) contributes to insulin resistance and endothelial dysfunction — individuals with hypertension should limit intake to one piece (≈45 g) and avoid pairing with other high-sugar foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~98) from glutinous rice flour skin combined with 11.2 g sugar/100 g and only 2.1 g dietary fibre/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glutinous rice flour base carries one of the highest GI values among grain products (GI ≈ 98), causing rapid post-prandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Ang Ku Kueh and, if consumed, restrict to half a piece (≈22 g) paired with a low-GI meal to partially blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g estimated), high sugar (11.2 g/100 g), and possible trans fat from vegetable shortening or lard used in traditional preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of added sugar (11.2 g/100 g) and possible saturated or trans fat from lard or shortening raises concern for dyslipidaemia and atherosclerotic risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and verify the fat source used in preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ang Ku Kueh (Sweet Bean) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese steamed glutinous rice cake shaped to resemble a red tortoise shell, symbolising longevity and good fortune in Hokkien and Teochew culture. The outer skin is made from glutinous rice flour mixed with vegetable shortening or lard and red food colouring, giving it a soft, sticky, and slightly chewy texture. The filling consists of sweetened mung bean paste (sometimes labelled as sweet bean paste), which is smooth, mildly sweet, and dense. Each piece is typically steamed in a banana leaf-lined mould and weighs approximately 40–50 g. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 208 kcal, 37.1 g carbohydrate, 5.1 g protein, 4.4 g fat, 11.2 g sugar, 2.1 g dietary fibre, and 64 mg sodium. The glutinous rice flour base has a very high glycemic index (GI ≈ 98), making this food a high-glycaemic load snack unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The sugar content of 11.2 g/100 g is notable for hypertension management. It is commonly consumed as a festive snack during Chinese New Year, birthdays, and religious ceremonies, and is widely available at traditional kueh stalls across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F11090003) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous glutinous rice cake entries. GI is estimated from peer-reviewed analogous glutinous rice data (Foster-Powell et al., 2008). Overall confidence is high for the HPB-reported macros and moderate for the estimated saturated fat and GI.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous glutinous rice cake / mung bean paste entries as HPB does not report saturated fat for this item; this value should be updated when HPB publishes a full fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean glutinous rice cake are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 98 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous (waxy) rice GI values from Foster-Powell et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement for Ang Ku Kueh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 4. Halal status is marked false because traditional recipes may use lard; halal-certified versions exist but cannot be assumed for a generic record. 5. Vegan status is marked false because lard is a common traditional fat source. 6. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'malay' which appears to be a data entry error; Ang Ku Kueh is a Hokkien/Teochew Chinese cake adopted into Singaporean multicultural food culture — corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese (Peranakan-influenced)' with ethnic_tags retaining both chinese and malay.
